speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 23rd of July, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about the Germans coming through your town on the way to Greece. Had you heard-- you say that when they came to the shops with their so-called ration cards, the [INAUDIBLE] Jewish shop owners just gave them what they wanted to. Had you already heard at that time what the Germans were doing to Jews in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard it, yes. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- see, my uncle was a lithographist, had his own color printing. He even printed false documents for some people who succeeded to get away from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, John? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leiberman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leiberman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandor. That's in English, Alex.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex Leiberman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leiberman. We, we had a number of people from Poland who were given-- everybody had to have a police document that, that you are a citizen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So these people who came from Galicia and, and other occupied territories of Poland, they were given a false certificate and also were kept in the synagogue, in a large synagogue. And, and they had other building next to the synagogue. Because they couldn't speak the language, they were told not to rely on that certificate only, because they, they can be caught. If someone checks people in the street, and you can't speak the language, doesn't matter if you had that false certificate, which looked, looked real, was like a real.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they picked up the language after six months. Some survived. Some were caught. So we knew. Do you remember these-- seeing these people in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a few, yes, because I knew, I went in the-- one of the rooms where they feed-- they were at-- they were given food, were brought food, and all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who fed them, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: People from the community. It was a Jewish community of 15,000 to 20,000, with the area around that Oradea, was over 15,000 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you managed to keep their, their presence quiet? People didn't hear about them, know about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know about them because your uncle was making false documents for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in the house. And was the shul nearby to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the shul was about a good kilometer from me, from where we lived, where my uncle lived. But we knew about them, because they were-- they never had children with them. They, they took off by themselves, each-- they could run. They must have come out from some concentration camps, even, because they told us what happened to them, and what happens to other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the stories? 'Cause, I mean, you were then a boy of 15, 16.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember vaguely. [BACKROUND NOISE] And I remember also some people who lived in Hungary, but their ancestors came from Galicia, were expelled. They had expulsion orders. And they couldn't even speak another language, only Hungarian. But they were expelled and taken to the border, and, and let them go over the border, even a couple of shots in the air to make them run faster. The other side was already occupied by Germany, so what happened to them--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know. But you knew that they were, the Jewish people in the rest of Europe were suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew, but see, I was a more or less [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had access to a shortwave radio. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Radios were-- shortwave radios who were held by Jewish people had to be sealed in the back and set to-- only to the Hungarian government station. So you couldn't have the dial moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only, only Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- so they couldn't listen in to London or Moscow during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would they know if you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they come and check you occasionally. And then you could get six months' imprisonment for not sealing your radio onto the one station. So what happens frequently, when I went on the summer holiday home to our village, no one had a radio in- in the village. There was only one official who had a radio, a Hungarian man. And that was operating on batteries, car batteries. And I went to Szilagy-Somlyo that 18 kilometers away, on a pushbike. Took me about three quarters of an hour to an hour. And I had to memorize the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents instructed you to listen to a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the community in the synagogue, where I had to come back and tell the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't write it down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They could catch me with, with, with the writings, like how many bombs they delivered in various cities in West Germany in the rural, different cities, and where the front line is, because from the local papers, you could only figure out the front line, especially on the Russian front, three weeks-- what, what happened three weeks earlier they announced yesterday. That went normally.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that stage, when you were hearing these broadcasts and you were-- other secret information and from these people who were locked up in the synagogue, did your parents not want to leave Hungary at the time? You were part of Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my parents in the village couldn't leave. They were hard to move. There's no--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt and uncle that you lived with? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't talk of leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. They actually-- my uncle had a, a German partner, actually was a volksdeutsch who lived in Hungary. And he could have his lithography only if he had a non-Jew partner. So this German was a good partner. He actually wanted to hide him. He had wine-- what do you call, grape. He produced a bit of wine.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had vines.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had grapes in the hills. But they never took up the offer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My auntie did. My uncle didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting. Now, John, what happened when Germany occupied Hungary, when Germany came into Hungary? How did that-- how did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had regulations. We finished school and sent home, and...

speaker: interviewer
text: Finished school. What, what year were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you in? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sixth form.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the sixth-- in the sixth form. It was only March, halfway through the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the normal year ends approximately on the 20th of June. And it was approximately the 20th of March, or 20-29 of March, I think, when the Germans came in, end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And they sent the children from the school. They all sent you all home.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent straightaway home the, the children who were from provinces, not from the city. And after a week, they closed the whole school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went home to your parents at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the end of March, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they order-- they ordered the deportation, the Hungarian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about four weeks, four or five weeks after, sometimes in April. They read-- two military police appeared, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: In your little village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they- they appeared, the local military police. And they read government's rule, that...

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did it say? What, what were-- what were the conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that they have to remove us, and, and not to return to this place at all, ever. So they didn't-- weren't specific. Just you had to give them any valuables.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give them your valuables.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And- and-

speaker: interviewer
text: And money?

speaker: interviewee
text: They- Money and valuables. And you could take small carriage-- what you can carry with you. And we were taken to the synagogue next door to us. And all Jewish families from other villages were there for about two days. And then we were taken by drawn horse carriages to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the Somlyo-- Szilagy-Somlyo that, that town 18 kilometers away, which they had a disused brick factory, which had a railway line come to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me-- can you spell the name of, of the place where the brick factory was? Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Szilagy-Somlyo. S-Z-I-L-A-G-Y dash Somlyo, S-O-M-L-Y-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, when they-- when they came to your house, did they go to each, each person's house, the military police?

speaker: interviewee
text: To that, that's-- actually, they were gendarmes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, from your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from your village. And they went to each Jewish person's house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of each of the 12 or 15 families and told them to take their belong-- whatever they could carry. Do you remember what you took, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I only took some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some clothing. It was-- we were heading for spring. It was spring, practically, already. And I didn't take too many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything special that you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I remember one thing. They-- I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What I took, I don't remember. But what I didn't take, I remember more. They took my watch, a Certina chrome Swiss watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hid my bike. I had a, a bike, a German Miele. They produced bikes those days. Now they produce [LAUGHS] dishwashers, mainly. And I even cut the tires.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tires of your bike.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wouldn't be able to drive it away. What did your mother take, John? Do you remember what she took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- they confiscated the jewelry. We had to give what she had on her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take things like candlesticks or anything? Or You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. I don't think she took with her-- they were-- they were actually waiting for you to pack and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stood over you while--

speaker: interviewee
text: They stood over you, yeah. It was a matter of half an hour, and off you-- off you go. You signed some--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you- and they took you all into the synagogue next door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the synagogue, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people from other villages. And they kept you there for a couple of days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they transported you to, to the-- to the brick-- disused brick-

speaker: interviewee
text: To that brick-- disused brick factory, which was-- they set up as a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you get from your village to that-- to that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: By horse-drawn carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Open carriages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Open carriages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you off there. When you got there, how many people did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were about 2,000 people there, from the whole area, from roughly up to about 100 kilometers away. That was a whole shire, like they had about 70-- the whole country was divided into about 70 districts. Each had their own judiciary. Actually, the man in charge for, for, for that ghetto, the Hungarian man, he was the chief justice of this area. [LAUGHS] And the ghetto had only two German soldiers. All the rest were Hungarians, gendarmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the-- was it bricked off from the rest of the town? This--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was close to a river, a small river. And no people lived there close by. The town would have been about three, four kilometers from, from the brick factory. But the brick factory has its own railway tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And did it have a fence around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the factory had a fence, but we couldn't be accommodated in the factory. We were sleeping in the open fields next to the factory. Actually, it was open fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: No shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the-- all the time that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- some women had shelter in the-- next to the kiln. They had storages. But I was in the open.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two weeks, that was the beginning of April 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Up till about mid-April--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that known to be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: --or, or end of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was known to be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was made into a ghetto when these orders came through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes...Yes, that made it into a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was for all this district of-- called Szilagy. That's the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jewish people, wherever they lived in this area, came to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all-- you could all speak Hungarian, so everybody could communicate with one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the-- your life in the ghetto for the few weeks you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] volunteered [LAUGHS] to cut some timber for the-- for the kitchen, which supplied the food for the-- for the guards, the gendarmes. We got some extra food for cutting the timber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were with you all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your aunt and uncle in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were in a different ghetto in the same town where the school was. It was a much bigger center. But when I cut wood, these two people who survived with me-- they're in Israel. The three of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your school friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not school friends, only school friends from primary school. They didn't come in town. One is deaf and dumb now. And I visited him three years ago. And the other one-- the three of us, we cut wood. So we saw what's going on there. They did a lot of-- they try to force all sorts of things out of different people. They thought they can-- they can get their jewelry. Some have. Some didn't even have it where they hid it. And they were beating them up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was beating them? The Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian, Hungarian gendarmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Hungarian police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Hungarian. And they had all sorts of ways of making them talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I saw a young woman, probably in her early 20s. They, they put under her knee a broom stick.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the back of her knees... Yes..

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back of her knees, which went through her two arms cross, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it went-- her arms had to go--

speaker: interviewee
text: The arms had to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: --through the broom.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the knees up, and the stick through here. And then your hands were tied up with string. And you actually, if they hit her, she were moving like a ball. She couldn't stretch, not foot, not, not hands. And some men were tied with their hands in the back, so the tip of their toe was reaching the ground, all their weight. And they would pull them up on some live trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hung them from trees by--

speaker: interviewee
text: From trees. So they're only 10 meters away. They're hanging them up there. And, and the weight of them were on the shoulder. They could be conscious up to 10 minutes, and then they lost consciousness.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the hands were tied at the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their hands-- and the rope from the hands--

speaker: interviewee
text: The rope from the hand was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the-- pulled up on the tree until the weight-- all their weight was on, on the tip. They were not fully in the air. Tip on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tip of their feet touched the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they passed out usually between 10 and 15 minutes. And then they let them loose. And they poured a bucket or two of cold water over their head, so they revived. And either they let them go, and next day they started again, or they even started-- they put them up straightaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had access to all these things because you were working in the kitchens.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I worked in the open, next to the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just cut wood there. And I saw it. I was close by. And they were not-- they didn't do it in masses. They took out one or two at a time, and- and only people which they saw they are rich, or they didn't disclose whatever they had, or, or they had personal--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they trying to get from them, besides money or jewelry? What were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took the-- who took it, the Hungarian police or the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they were allowed to keep it for themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, there were only two German soldiers there. They were more or less in the background. But they probably told them what to do, because two Germans couldn't keep over 2,000 people. They had to have Hungarian guards there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember from your stay in the ghetto, John? How did your parents manage? You got extra food, because you-- you took the wood to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a bit of extra food, plus we had some dry food-- dry food [BACKROUND NOISE] from when we were taken from the synagogue to the ghetto. We went like a long caravan. Maybe was about-- were about 30 carriages. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were handed food from peasants from the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your own village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the villages that you passed through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were only two more villages to be passed through. But no, we -we got-- I remember I got sausages from-- smoked sausages from Romanian peasants, mainly Romanians.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you passed through their village, they gave it to you. And you managed to keep that for a few days while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we, we could keep it or eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get extra food because you were bringing wood into the kitchens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we got extra bread. But in the ghetto, they didn't supply food for the people. Everybody had to have some dry food to keep them going for two weeks. There was no, no food, like a kitchen, supplies, anything, even tea or coffee. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Of course, there was no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was no running water there, just a well and the river. And of course, they had to build some temporary toilet facilities, which were just along, along the lines of timber, with, with a bit of a fence in between male and female toilets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they, just holes in the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: It weren't even a hole. It was a trench. And no, no cubicles or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a fence with-- it was a trench with partitions in it, so that people could be separated from one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: No separation. It was just a long--

speaker: interviewer
text: A long trench for men and a long trench for women.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They were close to each other. But they-- one couldn't see the other. Like, they had a sort of a fence between them. But each could see each other inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Were you guarded a lot while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they, they guarded it all, all around the ghetto, Hungarian guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you have escaped if you wanted to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have escaped if I wanted to. But I don't think my parents could have. They..

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you were there-- if you were there on your own, would you have tried to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have escaped my own, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody else try to escape from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know anybody who, who escaped from that ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at the end of the two weeks or three weeks that you spent in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- they brought train, train carriages. And they started deporting us. I was among the first. There was a few transports going there. I was, I think, in the second. The first went, and next day, I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me together. We went-- they, they were Hungarian guards on cattle trains. , and they had only a small window, a small opening, a good strain, with barbed wire over it. And the doors were shut. And they tell a story that you're going to-- you won't-- you won't be taken out of the country, and all stories like that, so they wouldn't cause any panic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were taken to a town on the-- Hungarian town which was once Slovakia called Kass-- Kosice-- Kass.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A- double S, or the Czech name now is Kosice, K-O-S-I-C-E. And at, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the railway station, they-- we could get off the train, one person, if you had any dish, to collect some water, fresh water, drinking water. And then we only stopped for about 15 minutes. And then they changed the guards there. The Hungarians retreated, and the German guards took over, and moved off to, to Birkenau, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the trains very full?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very full.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all Hungarian and Romanian Jews were on those trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanian Jews were not deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the guards on the train were not near the carriages that you were in. They were-- the guards that traveled with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they were not on the train. They were on a separate-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they had guard, little guard cubicles on the train. They were there. And they had a carriage-- passenger carriage for the guards only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the German guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they stop and, and add any carriages to your train as you moved along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you repeat it, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: As you-- as your train moved along, you had separate carriages, cattle trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as the train moved along, did you stop anywhere? And did hey add any carriages to your train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a full load from, from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the disused brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: --brick factory right through.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you travel? Do you have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] about one night and part of two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was-- and there was no food given to you in that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the little bit of water you could get at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that little bit of water had to be for everybody in the carriage, in the, in the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then when you came to the end of the-- end of your-- when you-- when you came to this-- to the town, the Hungarian guards left. The German guards came and saw you off to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived there, former prisoners opened the doors, who were working for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, in striped uniform. And everyone had to get off the train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lined up five in a row, male separately, and females separately, and go to a selection table. And a German officer was looking at everybody and having a small stick, about-- could be 18 inches. And he was waving right or left. Those who were fit to work, he signaled to go to the right. And the same system was another officer at another table for the women. So...

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were fully clothed at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And wearing your own clothes from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our own clothes. My mother was quite young. She was 42. And she was sent to the left, because she kept-- [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] [CRYING] she kept the small child of someone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she do that, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a good friend-- she was a good friend of hers, district doctor's wife. And this doctor himself was from Czech territory, Hungarian-attached Czechoslovakia. And he was doing sort of a military service and appointed to our village, and to seven villages as a-- as a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was left at home alone. And his wife and three children were deported. And she was a much younger woman than my mother. But she had three children. And she couldn't line up there with three kids, so my mother helped her. She took one child. How old was the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: The small one could have been, her mother held, about a year and a half. And she had another one two and a half, and another one three and a half or four, three kids. And the Germans didn't separate mothers from babies or children. So even if she would have fitted for work, if she had someone else's child, they would send her to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of the selection before you came there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the first you head about it when you actually saw it happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- and she kept the child, your-- did she have to carry the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she carried the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: She carried her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She carried one. And her friend, the doctor's wife, carried the other two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the doctor's wife also sent to the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both, both of them. And they, they told, told us that in, in the weekend, the families will be reunited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course you all believed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you believe it or you not. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you couldn't do much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father at the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he was sent with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- did you see your mother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask about them, what had happened to them? Could you ask? Was there anybody you could inquire of?

speaker: interviewee
text: After next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wrote a letter. I got hold of a small pencil with one of the-- what you call them? Outside of a tree. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bark.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bark. There were some bits of white barks. They were a particular tree that grow in Poland, in Russia, completely white bark. You could peel it off and write on it like on paper. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote a few words. And I throw it over to a camp which accommodated children who were at the age of 6-- between 6 and 15, girls and boys. And they were even-- some were even praying with-- with [INAUDIBLE] in the morning. The Germans kept them. Whenever a Swiss Red Cross comes to visit, they will show they have a special camp for the kids. That camp was between, between our camp, which was called zigeunerlager, gypsy camp, because German gypsies were in charge of each barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I ask that kid to throw it over to the next camp, where our women were. And my auntie, who survived, she got that note from-- she told me after she got liberated, when we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you write in the note, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few words that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whatever they told [? her, ?] that we can see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good, thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape two of the interview with John Ullmann. It's the 23rd of July, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
